title: Open Source AI for Enterprise Workloads

modules.title: The Open Source AI Landscape: Models, Licenses, and What 'Open' Actually Means
modules.sub: Mapping the models, licenses, and real openness distinctions that matter
modules.description: Learners survey the current open source AI ecosystem, distinguishing between truly open models and those with restrictive or misleading licensing. By the end of this module, they can categorize prominent models like Llama and Mistral by their actual openness, commercial viability, and governance implications for enterprise use.

modules.title: Evaluating Open Source Models Against Enterprise Requirements
modules.sub: Applying structured criteria to match models to organizational needs
modules.description: Learners build a practical evaluation framework that weighs capability benchmarks, licensing terms, community health, and support options against specific enterprise requirements. They apply this framework to a set of candidate models and produce a shortlist with documented rationale for inclusion or exclusion.

modules.title: Deployment Architectures: On-Premise, Private Cloud, and Hybrid Approaches
modules.sub: Choosing the right infrastructure model for self-hosted AI workloads
modules.description: Learners compare on-premise, private cloud, and hybrid deployment patterns for running open source models, weighing latency, control, scalability, and operational complexity. They map each architecture pattern to representative enterprise scenarios and identify which deployment model best fits a given workload profile.

modules.title: Security, Compliance, and Data Governance with Self-Hosted Models
modules.sub: Managing risk, data residency, and compliance when hosting your own models
modules.description: Learners examine the security attack surface introduced by self-hosted models and identify the controls needed to satisfy common regulatory frameworks such as GDPR, HIPAA, and SOC 2. They produce a governance checklist that maps deployment decisions to compliance obligations and data residency requirements.

modules.title: Total Cost of Ownership: Infrastructure, Talent, and Maintenance Trade-offs
modules.sub: Calculating the true cost of open source AI beyond licensing savings
modules.description: Learners construct a total cost of ownership model that accounts for hardware or cloud compute, engineering labor, ongoing maintenance, and hidden operational overhead. They compare this TCO against equivalent proprietary API spending to produce a credible financial trade-off analysis for stakeholder review.

modules.title: Fine-Tuning and Customization for Domain-Specific Workloads
modules.sub: Adapting open source models to perform reliably in specialized enterprise domains
modules.description: Learners evaluate fine-tuning approaches — including full fine-tuning, LoRA, and prompt engineering — against the data availability, compute budget, and performance targets of a domain-specific use case. They design a customization plan that specifies method, dataset requirements, evaluation criteria, and expected performance lift.

modules.title: Integrating Open Source Models into Existing Enterprise Pipelines
modules.sub: Connecting self-hosted models to data, applications, and workflows already in place
modules.description: Learners identify the integration points — APIs, orchestration layers, data pipelines, and observability tooling — required to embed an open source model into a realistic enterprise architecture. They produce an integration design that addresses authentication, versioning, fallback handling, and monitoring for a chosen workload.

modules.title: Building an Open Source AI Strategy: Vendor Independence and Risk Management
modules.sub: Turning model-level decisions into a durable organizational AI strategy
modules.description: Learners synthesize the course frameworks into a structured open source AI strategy that addresses vendor lock-in risk, governance, build-versus-buy decisions, and long-term model maintenance. They deliver a deployment recommendation document with supporting trade-off analysis suitable for presentation to technical leadership or an architecture review board.

synopsis: Closed AI vendors offer convenience but introduce dependency, opacity, and data risks that are increasingly unacceptable for enterprise workloads. This course gives technical leaders and architects the frameworks to evaluate, deploy, and govern open source models — from Llama to Mistral — as credible alternatives or complements to proprietary APIs.
rationale: As enterprises mature their AI strategies, the build-vs-buy decision around foundation models is becoming a board-level concern — and most professionals lack the practical grounding to make it rigorously.
learning_outcome: Professionals can assess open source model options against their organization's security, cost, and capability requirements and produce a deployment recommendation with supporting trade-off analysis.
